children.title: Contents
children.summary: Contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 Adult Social Care accelerator guide. Lists front matter and the main chapter structure: overview, installation, case worker scenario, application analysis, and building the application, with subtopics such as prerequisites, evidence, products, intake process, wizard creation, person match, and workflows. Also includes notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Adult Social Care accelerator
1.1 Overview of Adult Social Care accelerator
The Adult Social Care business scenario
children.summary: This section introduces the Adult Social Care accelerator as a simple Cúram-based social program example. It explains the business goal of paying benefits to incapacitated individuals, using John’s scenario to illustrate Sickness Benefit and automatic transition to Invalidity Benefit after six months. It also describes the workflow for expiring practitioner certificates and lists core caseworker requirements such as client search/registration, application creation, evidence management, approval, eligibility and entitlement viewing, payment generation, certificate extension, and reassessment after changes of circumstance.

children.title: Adult Social Care example requirements
children.summary: This section outlines the Adult Social Care accelerator as a basic social program application on the Cúram Platform, using a worked example of benefit delivery for incapacitated clients. It describes the scenario, including Sickness Benefit, automatic transition to Invalidity Benefit after six months, and a workflow where a case owner extends an expiring practitioner’s certificate. It lists core caseworker requirements: client search/registration, application creation and approval, evidence management, eligibility and entitlement viewing, benefit payment generation, certificate extension, and change-of-circumstance reassessment.

children.title: The Adult Social Care business process
children.summary: Describes the Adult Social Care business process in Cúram: a client and caseworker gather and submit client information, which is assessed against business rules to determine eligibility for Adult Social Care benefits. It explains that the Cúram Platform supports this workflow through Cúram Case Management for identifying high-risk, high-need people and intervening, Cúram Intake for a flexible benefit intake process, and Cúram Intelligent Evidence Gathering for dynamic questionnaire-based information collection.

children.title: Adult Social Care design decisions
children.summary: This section outlines the main Adult Social Care design decisions the team must make, including initial system configuration, organization structure, user application views, intake, case management, evidence types, case ownership, eligibility determination, and financial processes. It notes that detailed analysis is covered later in “Analyzing the Adult Social Care application,” while build tasks are covered in “Building the Adult Social Care application.” It also states the accelerator is downloadable only to Merative members, but the sample application can be reproduced from the default product.

Introduces the Adult Social Care accelerator as a simple Cúram social program example and worked application workflow. It explains the goal of paying benefits to incapacitated individuals, using John’s case: Sickness Benefit after a doctor’s diagnosis and practitioner certificate, then automatic transition to higher-rate Invalidity Benefit after six months. It also covers a certificate-extension task for case owners, core caseworker needs (search/register clients, create/approve applications, manage evidence, view eligibility/entitlement, generate payments), and the Cúram components used: Case Management, Intake, and Intelligent Evidence Gathering.

children.title: 1.2 Installing the Adult Social Care accelerator
Prerequisites
children.summary: Before installing the accelerator, you must set up a Cúram Application Development Environment (ADE) for application development, including a Java IDE and IBM Rational Software Architect Designer, and install the Cúram Platform. This section is a prerequisite note for the Adult Social Care accelerator installation.

children.title: Installing and building the accelerator
children.summary: This section explains how to install and build the Adult Social Care accelerator. It lists prerequisites: set up a Cúram ADE with a Java IDE and IBM Rational Software Architect Designer, and install Cúram Platform. It then gives the build steps: extract the accelerator into %CURAM_DIR%, update environment scripts, add AdultSocialCare, IncapacityBenefit, and ASCIntakeConfiguration to component order and EAR packaging, run server and client build commands, then refresh the ADE workspace and start the application.

children.title: Starting the XML server
children.summary: Explains how to start the XML server in the ADE before running the Cúram application: go to `%CURAM_DIR%\CuramSDEJ\xmlserver` and run `ant -f xmlserver.xml`. Notes the default `CURAM_DIR` locations on Windows and Linux. Also states the XML server must be running before performing the Adult Social Care caseworker scenario.

This section explains how to install and build the Adult Social Care accelerator. It notes the accelerator is available only to Merative/Lab Services developers, and that installation requires a Cúram ADE with a Java IDE and IBM Rational Software Architect Designer, plus Cúram Platform. It then outlines the build process: extract the accelerator to %CURAM_DIR%, update environment scripts, adjust component order and EAR packaging, run server and client build commands, refresh the ADE workspace, and start the application.

children.title: 1.3 Performing the Adult Social Care case worker scenario
Adding a person
Searching for an existing person
children.summary: Explains how to search for an existing person in the Adult Social Care caseworker scenario. It instructs the user to log in as ascewuser, open Search for a Person from Quick Links, enter first and last name on the Person Search tab, and select Search. From the results, choose a Person link to open that person’s home page. Also notes that the XML server must be running before performing the scenario.

children.title: Registering a person
children.summary: Explains how to register a new person in Cúram: log in as ascewuser, use Quick Links > Register a Person, then enter required fields such as first/last name, gender, date of birth, registration date, marital status, nationality, country/region of birth, and address. Notes supported address formats are US, UK, and free form. Also begins a related Adult Social Care application topic describing person search/match behavior and criteria, including alternate ID list, date of birth, and first name with weights.

Explains how to add a person in the Adult Social Care caseworker scenario by either searching for an existing person or registering a new one. It notes the XML server must be running first. For an existing person, log in as ascewuser, use Quick Links > Search for a Person, enter first and last name on the Person Search tab, and open a returned Person link to view the home page.

children.title: Applying for Adult Social Care
Application case values for Adult Social Care
children.summary: This section lists the exact application case values for an Adult Social Care test scenario. It includes claimant details such as James Smith, address in Fairfield, Utah, email, preferred language, interpreter need, SSN, date of birth, gender, marital status, citizenship, household date, residency status, and living arrangement. It also references the application workflow steps for reviewing answers and submitting the form. Useful for locating the required data values when creating or verifying an Adult Social Care application case.

This section explains how to create and submit an Adult Social Care application case in Cúram. It covers registering/searching the claimant, entering claimant details, reviewing answers, and completing practitioner certificate and diagnosis steps. It also documents the exact example case values for James Smith, including address, email, preferred language, SSN, DOB, gender, marital status, citizenship, residency, and living arrangement, plus workflow navigation to the submitted case.

children.title: Authorizing the Adult Social Care application
children.summary: This section explains how to authorize an Adult Social Care application in Cúram. Authorizing the application creates both an associated integrated case and a product delivery case, and shares all evidence from the application to the integrated case. It also gives the basic procedure: use the ACTIONS menu, click Authorize, optionally enter comments in the Authorize Program window, then click Save.

children.title: Activating the product delivery case
children.summary: This section explains how to activate a product delivery case from an integrated case, review eligibility and entitlement determinations, and generate a payment through the Financials > Transactions tab. It also introduces the Adult Social Care workflow, noting that a payment must be generated first before triggering workflow tasks. The section is useful if you need steps for activating the case, viewing decision details, issuing payment, or preparing to trigger certificate renewal/extension workflow tasks.

children.title: Generating a payment
children.summary: This section explains how to generate a payment on a product delivery case: open Financials > Transactions, click Issue Payment, confirm, and refresh to view the transaction. It also introduces the next Adult Social Care workflow steps, including activating the product delivery case, reviewing determinations, and a workaround for triggering workflow tasks by overriding the server date. Useful if you need payment creation or workflow-trigger setup details.

children.title: Performing the Adult Social Care workflow
Triggering the Adult Social Care workflow
children.summary: Explains a workaround for triggering the Adult Social Care workflow. It assumes the previous “generate a payment” step is complete, then shows how to override the application’s server date so you can set a future date and generate a scheduled payment. This is used to trigger workflow tasks for extending and renewing a practitioner certificate. Useful if you need to understand how to initiate workflow processing in the demo.

children.title: Performing the Adult Social Care workflow task
children.summary: Describes the Adult Social Care workflow task for extending a practitioner certificate after payment generation creates the case owner’s task. It explains how to find the available task, take ownership, navigate to the evidence workspace, edit the Practitioner Certificate evidence, update the Specified Certification To date, save, and apply changes to complete the task. This section is useful if you need the exact procedure for completing the workflow task and modifying the certificate evidence.

Covers the Adult Social Care workflow end-to-end: a workaround to trigger workflow processing by overriding the server date, then generating a scheduled payment to create workflow tasks. It also explains how to complete the case owner’s task for extending a practitioner certificate: find the “Practitioner certificate needs to be extended” task, take ownership, open the evidence workspace, edit the Practitioner Certificate evidence, update the Specified Certification To date, and apply changes.

children.title: Capturing changes in circumstance
Changing the Incapacity Benefit Product Type
children.summary: Explains how to change Incapacity Benefit product type evidence from Sickness Benefit to Invalidity Benefit, and the downstream impact on case determinations and payments, including higher payment rates and issuing updated payments/over- and under-payment records. Also introduces adding a practitioner certificate with new diagnoses to move a claimant from Sickness Benefit to Incapacity Benefit after six months, with procedures for doing this via dynamic evidence screens or the Evidence Management wizard.

children.title: Adding a practitioner certificate with new diagnoses
Adding evidence by using the dynamic evidence creation screens
Practitioner certificate and diagnosis values for a change in circumstance by using the evidence screens
children.summary: Explains how to record a change in circumstance using the evidence screens by adding a new practitioner certificate and diagnoses, then applying changes to update determinations. Key fields for the practitioner certificate include received date, claimant James Smith, medical practitioner Tony Taylor, certificate type Doctor, and certificate from date set one day after the previous certificate’s to date. Also describes adding diagnosis evidence with received date, diagnosis, and examination date, and notes that the resulting benefit decision changes are reflected on the Determinations tab.

Shows how to add a change in circumstance using dynamic evidence screens in Adult Social Care. The process creates a new practitioner certificate and one or more diagnoses via In-Edit Evidence > New Evidence, then applies changes so the case moves from Sickness Benefit to Incapacity Benefit after six months. Key values include claimant James Smith, medical practitioner Tony Taylor, certificate type Doctor, and a certificate from date one day after the previous certificate’s to date. The section also notes checking updated determinations after saving.

children.title: Adding evidence by using the Evidence Management wizard
Practitioner certificate and diagnosis values for a change in circumstance by using the Evidence Management wizard
children.summary: This section gives the input values for creating a practitioner certificate and diagnosis in the Evidence Management wizard for a change in circumstance in Adult Social Care. It lists the practitioner certificate fields (date reported, claimant James Smith, medical practitioner Tony Taylor, certificate type Doctor, from date one day after the prior certificate, to date 26 weeks later, examination date, signature date, claim form signed Yes) and the diagnosis values (received date today, diagnosis Paralysis). It supports updating benefits after completing the guided change process.

This section explains how to add evidence in Adult Social Care using the Evidence Management wizard via Guided Change > Add Medical Condition. It covers entering a practitioner certificate and one or more diagnoses, then finishing the wizard, completing the change, and applying the evidence to update benefits. It includes the key field values for the example: claimant James Smith, practitioner Tony Taylor, certificate type Doctor, dates based on today and the prior certificate, claim form signed Yes, and diagnosis Paralysis.

Explains how to add a practitioner certificate plus one or more diagnoses in Adult Social Care to extend sickness evidence past six months and trigger a move from Sickness Benefit to Incapacity Benefit. Covers two methods: dynamic evidence screens and the Evidence Management wizard (Guided Change > Add Medical Condition). Includes the example values for James Smith, Tony Taylor, certificate type Doctor, certificate dates based on the prior certificate, and diagnosis Paralysis, then applying changes and checking Determinations.

Explains how Adult Social Care captures changes in circumstance and the effect on determinations and payments. Covers changing Incapacity Benefit Product Type from Sickness Benefit to Invalidity Benefit, which increases payment rates and updates over/under-payment records. Also describes adding a practitioner certificate plus diagnoses to extend sickness evidence past six months and move a claimant to Incapacity Benefit, using either dynamic evidence screens or the Evidence Management wizard. Includes example values for James Smith, Tony Taylor, Doctor certificate, and diagnosis Paralysis.

This section is a full Adult Social Care caseworker walkthrough. It covers starting the XML server, adding or finding a person, creating and submitting an application, using the example claimant James Smith, authorizing the application, activating the product delivery case, generating payment, triggering workflow by overriding the server date, completing the “Practitioner certificate needs to be extended” task, and capturing changes in circumstance. It also shows two evidence-adding methods (dynamic screens and Evidence Management wizard) and example values for certificates and diagnoses.

children.title: 1.4 Analyzing the Adult Social Care application
Completing a fit gap analysis
Fit gap analysis scenario
children.summary: This section presents a fit gap analysis example for the Adult Social Care application. Business analyst Fiona compares customer requirements with default Cúram functionality, focusing on practitioner certificate details and the client’s home telephone number. It lists the required certificate data fields and explains that success depends on understanding default product features and reviewing product documentation. It also notes that phone numbers are managed as person evidence and points to the Participant Guide for more detail.

children.title: Analyzing the default functionality
Reading product information
children.summary: Explains how to analyze Adult Social Care default functionality against customer requirements using fit gap analysis. Highlights the need to understand Cúram product documentation before comparing business processes, with an example focused on practitioner certificate details and person phone details. Describes product documentation as a source of business/technical version-specific information, plus Redbooks and white papers, to understand default features. Notes that Fiona uses documentation on phone numbers to learn they are maintained as person evidence, with a pointer to the Participant Guide.

This section explains how to analyze Adult Social Care default functionality against customer requirements using fit gap analysis. It emphasizes starting with a solid understanding of Cúram product documentation before comparing business processes. The example focuses on practitioner certificate details and client home phone numbers, showing how Fiona uses product docs, Redbooks, and white papers to understand default features. It notes that phone numbers are maintained as person evidence and points to the Participant Guide for more information.

children.title: Evaluating the default application scenario
Complete the Adult Social Care application intake script
children.summary: This section shows Fiona evaluating the default Adult Social Care application intake script against project requirements. She creates an application case with client details, a practitioner certificate, and diagnosis data, then inspects evidence to find gaps: the practitioner certificate does not capture inpatient indicator or facility name. It also covers checking person phone evidence, where the layout fits but the label “Phone Number” differs from the required “Telephone,” and “Home” is missing from the phone type code table.

children.title: Examining the practitioner certificate evidence
children.summary: This section shows Fiona reviewing default Cúram application evidence to assess fit with Adult Social Care requirements. She checks the practitioner certificate evidence and finds gaps: inpatient indicator and facility name are not captured. It also briefly covers examining person phone evidence, where the layout mostly fits but the label “Phone Number” differs from required “Telephone,” and “Home” is missing from the phone type code table. Useful for understanding evidence gaps in the default application.

children.title: Examining person evidence
Cúram Analysis Documentation Tooling (CADT)
children.summary: CADT is a downloadable Cúram Support tool for technical users to generate application-specific analysis documentation. It helps business and technical users perform fit-gap and customization impact analysis by exploring metadata, business functions, and related artifacts such as pages, database tables, tabs, domain definitions, code tables, message files, application properties, and workflows. The section includes a worked example showing how Fiona uses CADT to search for “Phone,” inspect the New Phone Number page, identify a label mismatch (“Phone” vs “Telephone”), and drill into the Phone_Type_Code domain definition.

children.title: Analyzing requirements using the Cúram Analysis Documentation Tooling (CADT)
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Analysis Documentation Tooling (CADT) generates application-specific analysis documentation for technical and business users, supporting fit-gap and customization impact analysis. It shows how to use the documentation to inspect pages, tables, and related artifacts such as tabs, domain definitions, code tables, message files, application properties, and workflows. A worked example walks through searching for “Phone,” opening the New Phone Number page, identifying a label inconsistency (“Phone” vs “Telephone”), and drilling into the Phone_Type_Code domain definition.

This section shows Fiona evaluating person phone evidence in the Adult Social Care application. She opens Person > Evidence > New and checks the New Phone Number modal, confirming the layout fits but noting gaps: the label “Phone Number”/“Phone” should be “Telephone,” and “Home” is missing from the Phone Type code table. It then introduces CADT, explaining how it generates application-specific analysis documentation for fit-gap and customization impact analysis across pages, tables, tabs, domain definitions, code tables, message files, application properties, and workflows.

Fiona evaluates the default Adult Social Care application against project requirements. She finds gaps in practitioner certificate evidence: inpatient indicator and facility name are not captured. She also reviews person phone evidence; the layout fits, but the label uses “Phone”/“Phone Number” instead of “Telephone,” and “Home” is missing from the Phone Type code table. The section then introduces CADT, explaining how it generates application-specific analysis documentation for fit-gap and customization impact analysis across pages, tables, tabs, domain definitions, code tables, message files, application properties, and workflows.

This section explains how to complete a fit gap analysis for Adult Social Care. Fiona compares customer requirements with default Cúram functionality for practitioner certificate and person phone details, using product documentation and direct application review. It identifies gaps such as missing inpatient indicator and facility name on practitioner certificate evidence, and the “Phone”/“Phone Number” label plus missing “Home” phone type. It then introduces CADT, which generates application-specific analysis documentation for fit gap and customization impact analysis across pages, tables, domains, code tables, message files, properties, and workflows.

children.title: Analyzing the organization structure
Cúram organization structure
children.summary: Explains Cúram organization structure as the application’s logical representation of how an organization is physically structured. Covers organization units, positions, jobs, their relationships, and how users fit into the hierarchy. Notes that this structure creates a reporting hierarchy used throughout the application, especially for managing case management activities such as supervisory approval tasks across the organization. Refers readers to the Organization Administration Guide for more detail.

children.title: Defining the organization structure requirements
Defining the organization unit requirements
children.summary: Explains how to define organization units within an organization structure. Units should be built in levels to reflect departmental hierarchy and reporting lines, including regional administration where relevant. Gives an example of a regional unit with multiple divisional child units. This section sits within broader organization structure requirements and leads into job and position requirements, but focuses specifically on constructing the unit hierarchy.

children.title: Defining the job requirements
children.summary: Defines how to configure organization structure for work distribution in Cúram: create organization units in a hierarchy, define jobs, and define positions with reporting relationships. Jobs can be generic or specific and are linked to one or more positions for task allocation. Position reporting normally follows the organization unit hierarchy and lead positions, but can be customized with “reports to” and Position Search for cross-unit or nonstandard reporting structures.

children.title: Defining position requirements
Defining the lead position requirements
children.summary: Explains how to define lead position requirements in an organization structure. At least one lead position and one user in it are needed for basic reporting; otherwise reporting falls back to a parent organization unit’s lead position. Describes when to use multiple lead positions and how task escalation balances tasks among users in those positions. Also notes supervisor workspace implications for shared supervision and briefly introduces the Cúram location structure, which maps physical locations, supports security, public offices, and user access control.

children.title: Defining multiple lead position requirements
children.summary: Explains when to define multiple lead positions in an organization unit and how they affect task assignment. If multiple lead positions exist, tasks go to the least-loaded users in those positions; if no reports-to user exists, tasks go to the lead position user or parent unit lead. Notes that multiple lead positions may be needed for shared supervision and supervisor workspace grouping, and that users needing the same subordinate list should be in the same unit or under a parent unit.

Defines how to set up positions and reporting links within organization units. Positions should form a reporting hierarchy across and within units, normally inherited from organization unit structure and lead positions, but can also use explicit “reports to” assignments for cross-unit or nonstandard reporting. Notes that manual reporting hierarchies may better reflect supervision based on experience, and that Position Search lets a position report to any other position in the organization.

Defines the core organization structure requirements: build organization units in hierarchical levels to reflect departments, reporting lines, and regional administration; set up jobs for work distribution and associate them with one or more positions; and create position reporting hierarchies within and across units. It explains default reporting via unit hierarchy and lead positions, with optional “reports to” links and Position Search for cross-unit or nonstandard supervision.

children.title: Cúram location structure
children.summary: Describes Cúram’s location structure as the logical model of physical work sites, including parent-child relationships between locations. Explains that locations support security and control user access to case/client information, and that users are assigned a location which determines their access. Notes that some locations are public offices for participant interaction and registration. Also connects the surrounding topic to organization structure rules for lead positions, reporting, task assignment, and load balancing when multiple users or lead positions exist.

children.title: Defining the location structure requirements
Defining the location hierarchy requirements
children.summary: Explains how to define location hierarchy requirements for Location Based Security (LBS). It says the organization structure is the starting point, only one active location structure can exist, and each structure needs a root plus parent/child locations. Access to case/client data is determined by comparing user location (from user record or position) with case owner or primary client location. It also outlines LBS modes: Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only, and notes additional custom requirements may be needed for special visibility rules.

Defines how to set up location structure requirements for Location Based Security (LBS). It says the organization structure is the starting point, only one active location structure can exist, and each structure needs a root plus parent/child locations arranged to control case/client access across workplaces. It explains LBS modes: Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only. Access is based on comparing user location (from user record or position) with case owner or client location, and special visibility rules may require customization.

children.title: Defining the security requirements
Defining the organizational security requirements
children.summary: This section explains how to define Cúram deployment security requirements at three levels: organizational, location, and user. It describes SID-based access for organization units (Read, Maintain, Create Unit), noting Maintain and Create override Read. It also covers location security for case/client access and administration, with Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs and Maintain granting view access. Finally, it notes that user account setup should follow the organization structure and consider position/location, security role, and application view.

children.title: Defining the location security requirements
children.summary: This section explains how to define Cúram security requirements at organizational, location, and user levels. It covers SID-based access for organization units and locations, including Create, Read, and Maintain permissions and how Maintain/Create override Read. It also notes that locations can restrict access to case/client data and to location administration details. Finally, it introduces user security setup, using positions as a basis for user accounts and considering position/location, security role, and application view.

children.title: Defining the user security requirements
children.summary: This section explains the security requirements needed to deploy Cúram, covering organizational, location, and user setup. It describes how organization units use Read, Maintain, and Create Unit SIDs, with Maintain/Create overriding Read. It also outlines location security using Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs to control access to location administration and related case/client information. For user security, it notes that user account setup should follow the organization structure and consider positions, location, security roles, and application view.

This section states that Cúram deployment requires defining security requirements at three levels: organizational, location, and user. Organizational units use Read, Maintain, and Create Unit SIDs, with Maintain/Create overriding Read. Locations use Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs to control access to location administration and case/client information. User setup should follow the completed organization structure and be based on positions, location, security role, and application view.

Explains how to map an organization and its locations to Cúram artifacts. Covers organization structure as the logical model of units, positions, jobs, users, and reporting hierarchy; requirements for building units, jobs, positions, lead positions, multiple lead positions, load balancing, and cross-unit reporting. Describes location structure for physical work sites and Location Based Security, including root/parent/child locations, access rules, and LBS modes. Also outlines organizational, location, and user security SIDs needed for deployment.

children.title: Analyzing the user requirements
User application view
children.summary: This section explains how to define security roles and user application views by identifying who can see and update person data, payments, services, and other sensitive data. It recommends reviewing functionality areas and requirements, then mapping security identifiers to groups, with special attention to high-risk areas like bank accounts. It also defines a user application view as a role-specific interface showing relevant home pages, pods, sections, shortcuts, searches, and tabs, and notes that users must be registered with a security role and view.

children.title: User interface components
children.summary: This section explains how to analyze user requirements for security roles and application views. It lists key questions about who can see or update person data, payments, services, and other sensitive data, and notes special cases like bank account changes. It describes user application views as predefined role-based screens containing home pages, pods, sections, shortcuts, searches, tabs, and other UI components. It also states that users must be registered and assigned a security role and application view.

children.title: Users
children.summary: Explains how to define user security requirements and application views. Key considerations include who can see or update person, payment, service, and other data, plus special-risk areas like bank accounts. Advises analyzing functionality and existing requirements to assign security identifiers to groups, keeping security groups minimal but not overly granular, and retaining default ADMIN/SYSADMIN accounts. Defines a user as an employee who performs system tasks; new users must be registered and assigned a security role and user application view.

children.title: Default user application views
Administrator user application view
children.summary: Describes the administrator user application view in Cúram. It grants access to administrator configuration areas such as organization, location, case structure, rules, evidence, intelligent evidence gathering, and intake. The section also contrasts system administrator and eligibility worker views, noting their separate capabilities. It then explains how to define user application view requirements during deployment, including deciding on personalized pod pages, sections, tabs, shortcuts, searches, preconfigured vs user-added pods, custom pods, and limiting sections to fewer than five.

children.title: System administrator user application view
children.summary: Describes the default user application views in Cúram, focusing on the system administrator view. The system administrator can configure application properties, case audit selection queries, communication templates, batch processes, security settings, business intelligence reports, target systems, and CMIS. It also contrasts administrator and eligibility worker views, and explains that user application views are configurable by sections, pods, tabs, shortcuts, and searches. The section emphasizes defining user role requirements and limiting sections to fewer than five.

children.title: Eligibility worker user application view
children.summary: Describes the eligibility worker user application view in Cúram, the main default view for adult social care users. It is designed for processing applications and cases: searching/adding persons, processing applications, creating and managing cases, determining eligibility, authorizing benefits, and handling change-in-circumstance work. Notes that the view’s sections, pods, tabs, and shortcut panel are configurable. Also outlines how to define user application view requirements by identifying role-specific functional/data needs, including personalized pod pages, sections, tabs, shortcuts, and searches, with sections limited to under five.

Cúram’s default user application views include administrator, system administrator, and eligibility worker views. The administrator view provides access to organization, location, case structure, rules, evidence, intelligent evidence gathering, and intake configurations. The system administrator view covers application properties, audit queries, communication templates, batch processes, security, BI reports, target systems, and CMIS. The eligibility worker view supports processing applications and cases, including person search/add, eligibility, benefits, and change-in-circumstance work. The section also explains how to define role-specific view requirements, including pods, sections, tabs, shortcuts, searches, and keeping sections under five.

children.title: Define the user application view requirements
children.summary: This section explains how to define user application view requirements for a Cúram deployment. It highlights default views (administrator, system administrator, eligibility worker) and their typical functions. It also says to review user roles, functional areas, and existing requirements to determine needed features. Key design elements include personalized pod pages, sections, tabs, shortcuts, and searches. It emphasizes choosing which pods are preconfigured or customizable, considering custom pods, and limiting displayed sections to fewer than five.

Explains how to analyze security and UI requirements by identifying who can view or update person, payment, service, and other sensitive data, including high-risk areas like bank accounts. Describes mapping requirements to security groups and keeping groups minimal, while retaining default ADMIN and SYSADMIN accounts. Defines a user application view as a role-specific interface with home pages, pods, sections, tabs, shortcuts, and searches, and notes users must be registered with a security role and view. Also covers default views for administrator, system administrator, and eligibility worker.

children.title: Analyzing the intake process requirements
Intake
children.summary: Explains the intake process for adding clients and creating application cases. Covers searching/registering clients, creating an internal application form (IEG script), adding clients, search-and-match on submission, mapping captured data as in-edit evidence, and creating an open application case based on program configuration. Also describes authorization, which may create integrated and product delivery cases and share evidence when brokering is enabled. Notes that application cases manage client and program information and may require evidence for program authorization.

children.title: Application case
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Intake uses an application case to manage program applications. It covers tailoring the application UI for user roles, especially tabs, shortcut panel links, and search pages. It then describes the intake flow: searching/registering clients, completing an IEG application form, matching or adding clients, creating an open application case, and mapping submitted data as in-edit evidence. It also notes authorization, which can create an integrated case and product delivery case, and that the application case manages clients and programs.

children.title: Define the application case requirements
Define the ownership strategy
Adult Social Care ownership strategy
children.summary: This section explains how to define application case requirements in Cúram, especially ownership and authorization strategies. It says application cases can include timers, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, notes, meetings, attachments, communications, and optional PCR assessments or appeals. For ownership, cases may be assigned to an organization unit, position, user, work queue, or, for internal applications only, the current user. In Adult Social Care, internal applications are handled by the worker, and submitted applications are assigned to the Eligibility Worker role.

This section explains how to define application case ownership. It says case ownership can be set separately for internal and online applications, with assignment to an organization unit, position, user, or work queue; internal applications can also be assigned to the current user. In Adult Social Care, workers handle internal applications, and submitted applications go to the Eligibility Worker role. It also notes related application case requirements such as timers, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, notes, meetings, attachments, communications, PCR assessments, and appeals.

children.title: Define the program authorization strategy
Application level authorization strategy
children.summary: Explains application-level program authorization for an application case. If selected, all programs are authorized together via the case’s Authorize action, using a CER ruleset to determine eligible programs and clients. It also requires choosing whether authorization creates a new integrated case or uses an existing one; case participants and required evidence are copied as needed. The section sits within broader application case requirements, alongside ownership strategy options and related application features such as timers, eligibility checks, PCR assessments, appeals, and other case components.

children.title: Program level authorization strategy
children.summary: Explains how to define application case requirements, especially ownership and program authorization. Ownership can be assigned to an org unit, position, user, work queue, or current user for internal applications, with separate strategies for internal vs online cases. For authorization, the section contrasts application-level vs program-level strategies. Application-level authorizes all programs together and may create or use an integrated case, with CER rules determining eligible programs/clients. Program-level authorization applies when multiple program types are linked and uses separate integrated cases.

children.title: Adult Social Care authorization strategy
children.summary: This section explains Adult Social Care’s application processing design choices. Authorization is set at the program level for expediency, allowing each program to be authorized separately and optionally linked to integrated cases and product deliveries. It also describes eligibility check strategy decisions, including CER rule sets, entitlement output, XML storage considerations, and notes that eligibility checks were not enabled for Adult Social Care. Finally, it outlines workflow options for application case creation, withdrawal requests, and straight-through processing.

Defines how to set application case authorization at either the application level or program level. Application-level authorization approves all programs together via the case Authorize action, requires a CER ruleset to determine eligible programs and clients, and lets you choose whether to create a new integrated case or use an existing one, copying participants and required evidence as needed. Also frames this choice alongside ownership strategy options and related application case features.

children.title: Define the eligibility check strategy
Adult Social Care eligibility check strategy
children.summary: This section explains authorization and eligibility-check configuration for Adult Social Care application cases. It notes that authorization is set at the program level for expediency, and that eligibility check processing is not enabled on the Adult Social Care application case. It also highlights that eligibility checks would require a CER ruleset and that case determination XML storage can affect system space and performance. The section then introduces workflow options for application case creation, withdrawal requests, and straight-through processing.

This section explains how to configure eligibility checks for application cases. It says eligibility processing is optional, requires a CER ruleset and an Eligibility Check Strategy, and can return eligibility and entitlement decisions. It also warns that case determinations are stored as XML blobs, which can consume significant space and affect performance. For Adult Social Care, eligibility check processing is not enabled for expediency. It also introduces related workflow options for application case creation, withdrawal requests, and straight-through processing.

children.title: Define the workflow requirements
Adult Social Care workflow process
children.summary: Describes the Adult Social Care workflow triggered when an application case reaches Ready for Determination and the status is enabled. It notifies the case owner to extend a claimant’s certification period using a configurable day-based event, creates an inbox task, and closes it after evidence changes are applied. Also outlines defining evidence types and brokering for application cases, plus Adult Social Care dynamic evidence entities used for eligibility/entitlement rules, including household member, living arrangement, alien, and other configured evidence types.

This section explains which workflows can be configured for application case processing: Application Case Created, Withdrawal Request, and Straight Through. It describes when each workflow starts, including on case creation, online withdrawal requests, or straight-through handling from application submission to ongoing case creation and application closure. It also notes that straight-through processing only starts after all clients on the application case are successfully matched or registered.

children.title: Define the evidence types
Adult Social Care dynamic evidence types
children.summary: Describes Adult Social Care dynamic evidence configuration for an integrated case. It notes six dynamic evidence types, with Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, and Incapacity Benefit Product Type linked to the integrated case and used by associated product deliveries for eligibility/entitlement rules. Also outlines evidence brokering from the application case to integrated cases, product deliveries, and optionally person/prospect person. Example entity types shown include ASC Household Member, ASC Living Arrangement, and ASC Alien, with their core data purposes.

Explains how to define evidence types for an application case and enable evidence brokering to integrated cases, product deliveries, and optionally person/prospect person. In Adult Social Care, six dynamic evidence types are configured on the integrated case; Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, and Incapacity Benefit Product Type are used by product deliveries for eligibility and entitlement rules. Also describes workflow behavior for notifying the case owner to extend a claimant’s certification period.

This section outlines the main configuration decisions for an application case: ownership, program authorization, eligibility checks, workflows, and evidence types. It describes ownership options for internal vs. online applications, application-level vs. program-level authorization, and the CER/ integrated case implications. It also covers optional eligibility checking and its storage/performance impact, available workflows (created, withdrawal, straight-through, ready for determination), and evidence brokering. Adult Social Care uses program-level authorization, no eligibility checks, and specific workflow/evidence configurations.

children.title: Application intake script
children.summary: Describes the application intake script as the set of sections, question pages, questions, and conditional logic used to capture claimant information (for example: About the Claimant, Household, Expenses, Resources, Finish). Advises documenting requirements for any additions or modifications to existing intake scripts, so developers can update IEG files. Also introduces person search match: configurable search criteria and weights used to match new application clients to registered persons based on data such as name and date of birth.

children.title: Define the application intake script requirements
children.summary: Explains the application intake script and how to define its requirements. The script is built from sections, question pages, questions, and conditional logic to capture claimant information, with example sections like About the Claimant, Household, Expenses, Resources, and Finish. Advises deciding whether to modify the existing script or create a new one, then documenting any additions or changes as requirements for the developer to implement in the IEG files. Also introduces person search match criteria and weighted matching for application-to-registered-person searches.

children.title: Person search match
children.summary: Describes the application case creation search-and-match feature for linking a new application client to an existing registered person using configurable Person Search Match Criteria. Criteria are defined in the Participants administration section, and each criterion has a weight indicating its importance in the matching score. Example criteria include first name, last name, and date of birth with different weights used to calculate the returned person’s match score.

children.title: Define the person search match requirements
children.summary: This section explains how to define person search match requirements in Cúram. It says organizations must choose which search criteria to use and assign weights to each criterion; these settings determine whether a person is a conclusive, inconclusive, or non-match based on configured thresholds. It also briefly links to related person match modification tasks and then transitions into broader case management topics, including integrated case requirements and evidence types.

Covers intake and application-case setup requirements. Intake starts with client search/registration, then an IEG application form captures data, performs search-and-match against registered persons, creates an open application case, and stores submitted data as in-edit evidence; authorization may create integrated/product delivery cases and broker evidence. Defines application case configuration choices: ownership, program-level vs application-level authorization, eligibility checks, workflows, and evidence types/brokering. Also notes Adult Social Care choices and introduces application intake script structure and person search match criteria/weights.

children.title: Analyzing case management
Analyzing the integrated case requirements
Integrated case
children.summary: Describes the integrated case in Cúram 8.1.2 as the central view of a client and family’s needs, supporting products, services, ongoing case management, collaboration, and outcome plans. It explains that organizations must define evidence types, update and verification rules, evidence brokering between cases and persons, and case ownership strategy. Includes Adult Social Care dynamic evidence types, with examples such as Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, Incapacity Benefit Product Type, and ASC Household Member, and notes their use in eligibility and entitlement rules.

children.title: Defining evidence types
Adult Social Care dynamic evidence types
children.summary: This section explains how integrated case evidence types are defined, with emphasis on Adult Social Care dynamic evidence. It states that six dynamic evidence types are configured, and that Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, and Incapacity Benefit Product Type are linked to the integrated case type for use across product deliveries and eligibility/entitlement rules. It also begins listing dynamic evidence entities, describing ASC Household Member as a household resident record that can capture personal, citizen, veteran, and ethnic details.

children.title: Evidence updates
Defining evidence correction requirements
children.summary: Explains when to configure evidence correction versus succession. A correction replaces an existing evidence record with a new one to fix bad data, with Effective Date of Change left blank and no change over time; the user must end one record before adding another. Use correction when a person cannot have more than one instance of the evidence at a time, such as address history. Highlights that From Date and To Date capture the active period and that this choice is set in the Dynamic Evidence Editor.

children.title: Defining evidence succession requirements
children.summary: This section explains evidence succession as an update type where an existing evidence record changes over time, unlike correction. The Effective Date of Change determines whether an update is a succession or correction: if left blank, it is a correction; if changed, it indicates succession. It highlights that evidence types must be assessed for whether multiple instances can exist at once and whether From/To dates are needed. This helps decide how to model and manage evidence updates.

children.title: Adult Social Care evidence configurations
children.summary: Explains how Adult Social Care evidence is configured for updates and verification. It distinguishes correction-only evidence from evidence succession, where evidence changes over time via an Effective Date of Change. In Adult Social Care, Practitioner Certificate and ASC Diagnosis are correction-only and require end-dating before creating a new record, while Incapacity Benefit Product Type supports succession. Also introduces evidence verification, noting it is configured in administration and involves administration, case, and participant components.

Explains how evidence updates are handled and when to use correction versus succession. A correction replaces an incorrect record, leaves Effective Date of Change blank, and is used when only one instance can exist at a time; From/To dates capture the active period. Succession is used when evidence changes over time and the Effective Date of Change is updated. The section also gives Adult Social Care examples: correction-only Practitioner Certificate and ASC Diagnosis, and succession for Incapacity Benefit Product Type.

children.title: Evidence verification
Components of verification
Defining evidence verification requirements
children.summary: Explains how to define evidence verification requirements for an evidence type. It covers deciding whether attributes must be verified, and the key setup elements: verifiable data items, verification item utilizations, verification requirements, verification requirement usages, and dependent data items. It also notes that verifiable data items map to specific attributes within an evidence entity, verification requirement usages link requirements to case types, and verification items are the documents or proofs used to satisfy verification, such as a pay stub for income.

This section explains verification in evidence processing. It introduces the three components of verification: administration, case, and participant. Administration configures access, mandatory rules, and special processing; case/participant tools let caseworkers record and fulfill verifications. It also explains how to define evidence verification requirements by identifying which evidence attributes need verification and setting up verifiable data items, verification requirements/usages, verification items, and dependent data items. Examples include verifying income with a pay stub and linking requirements to specific case types.

children.title: Conditional verifications
Defining conditional verification requirements
children.summary: This section explains when and how to define conditional verification requirements for evidence types. It describes required configuration items: a mandatory rule class, optional display rule class, and optional display UIM. It emphasizes identifying evidence attributes that need conditional verification, especially cross-evidence rules (for example, Household Income depending on Dividends, Pension, or Wages and Salaries). It also advises documenting rules clearly, using flow diagrams and user scenarios, mapping evidence, and specifying user-facing results.

Conditional verifications are verification rules that trigger only when specified conditions are met, not simply when evidence is added or changed. The engine evaluates conditions at runtime and creates outstanding verification records only when required. Conditions may be based on the verifiable data item itself or on dependent evidence items whose values affect verification. Examples include earnings over a threshold, SSN-type alternate ID, or household income requiring verification when component incomes change.

Explains how evidence verification is configured and used. Covers the three verification components: administration (access, mandatory rules, special processing), case, and participant (caseworkers record and fulfill verifications). Defines key setup items: verifiable data items, verification requirements/usages, verification items/utilizations, and dependent data items, with examples like verifying income via pay stubs and applying rules by case type. Also describes conditional verifications, where the engine creates outstanding verification only when runtime conditions are met, including threshold- and dependent-evidence-based rules.

children.title: Evidence brokering
Defining evidence brokering requirements
children.summary: This section explains evidence brokering requirements: deciding whether evidence should be shared between cases and persons, and how evidence sharing/broadcast settings are configured. It highlights that agencies can define sharing rules by evidence type and case type, and asks whether the same evidence type appears on multiple case types and whether changes must be communicated to other cases. It follows related guidance on conditional verifications and documenting rules, mappings, and user-facing results.

children.title: Adult Social Care evidence brokering configurations
children.summary: Adult Social Care evidence brokering is enabled for identical evidence sharing on ASC Diagnosis, Incapacity Benefit Product Type, and Practitioner Certificate. Evidence is captured on the Adult Social Care application case and shared with the Social Care integrated case, with all relevant evidence types configured for brokering on the integrated case. The section also points to related configuration details and then introduces case ownership strategy, explaining workflow-based initial owner assignment via a Temporary Owner Assignment work queue and related logging/property settings.

This section explains evidence brokering and when evidence should be shared between cases or persons. It says agencies can configure evidence sharing/broadcast rules by evidence type and case type, and should decide whether the same evidence type is used across multiple case types and whether updates must be propagated. It also links this decision to documenting rules, mappings, and user-facing results for verification/conditional verification scenarios.

Defines how integrated case evidence types are configured: which dynamic evidence entities attach to the case, how they support eligibility/entitlement, and how updates work via correction vs succession (including Effective Date of Change). Covers Adult Social Care examples: Practitioner Certificate and ASC Diagnosis are correction-only; Incapacity Benefit Product Type supports succession. Also explains evidence verification setup (verifiable data items, requirements/usages, items/utilizations, dependent data items, conditional verification rules) and evidence brokering decisions for sharing evidence across cases/persons.

children.title: Defining the case ownership strategy
Assigning Case Ownership Using Workflow
children.summary: Explains the workflow-based Ownership Strategy for case types, which determines a new case’s initial owner via workflow and can route cases to specific work queues. When enabled, cases are first assigned to the Temporary Owner Assignment queue until workflow completes, then reassigned to the final owner. Also notes the related application property controlling whether temporary owner assignment events are logged. Useful if you need to configure or change case ownership behavior.

children.title: Initial Case Ownership Strategy
children.summary: Explains the default initial case ownership strategy and when it can be overridden by an Ownership Strategy setting. For integrated cases, ownership is assigned first to the primary client’s administrator, then the logged-in user, with validation failure if no active owner can be found. For product delivery cases, ownership is inherited from the related integrated case when possible, otherwise follows a similar fallback chain, including supervisor and organization unit. Also begins guidance on when to use Evidence Management wizards for recording changes in circumstance.

Explains how to choose and configure the case ownership strategy for a case type. The default Social Care integrated case and Incapacity Benefit product delivery case ownership can be changed via an Ownership Strategy setting that uses workflow to determine the initial case owner, including routing to a specific work queue. New cases are temporarily assigned to the Temporary Owner Assignment queue until workflow finishes, then reassigned. Notes the related application property for logging temporary owner assignment events.

children.title: Defining Evidence Management wizards for recording a change in circumstance
children.summary: Explains when to use an Evidence Management wizard to record a change in circumstance. It notes that changes may involve chained updates across multiple evidence types and can be handled either by direct evidence updates or a guided wizard process. The section’s purpose is to help decide whether a wizard is required and introduces the wizard as a step-by-step feature for managing complex change processes.

Explains how to analyze integrated case requirements in Cúram 8.1.2: choose evidence types attached to the integrated case, define correction vs succession update behavior via Effective Date of Change, configure verification rules and conditional verifications, decide evidence brokering/sharing across cases and persons, and set case ownership strategy using workflow or defaults. Includes Adult Social Care examples (Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, Incapacity Benefit Product Type, ASC Household Member) and notes when to use Evidence Management wizards for chained change-in-circumstance updates.

children.title: Analyzing the product delivery case requirements
Product delivery case
children.summary: Explains how to analyze product delivery case requirements for benefit eligibility and financial processing. Defines a product delivery case as the container for information and functions needed to determine eligibility and deliver benefits. Covers eligibility determination configuration, assigning Cúram Express Rules to benefit/liability products, and documenting Adult Social Care application rules. Lists key Incapacity Benefit rule set components: Non-Financial Requirements, Contribution Rules, Medical Certificate Rules, and Assistance Unit Determination, plus Sickness/Invalidity Benefit logic and default six-month Sickness Benefit before Invalidity Benefit.

children.title: Defining eligibility determination processing
Assigning Cúram Express Rules to products
Adult Social Care application rules
children.summary: This section documents Adult Social Care application rules for Incapacity Benefit eligibility and entitlement. It explains that the application rule set includes Non-Financial Requirements, Contribution Rules, Medical Certificate Rules, and Assistance Unit Determination. It also notes the Incapacity Benefit Product Type distinguishes Sickness Benefit from Invalidity Benefit, and that eligible clients typically receive Sickness Benefit for six months before automatically moving to Invalidity Benefit. Useful for understanding whether existing eligibility/entitlement rules need modification or extension.

children.title: Defining display rule requirements
Adult Social Care display rules
children.summary: Explains how Adult Social Care decision display rules are designed and configured in Cúram Express Rules. Decision details are product-specific, shown on dynamic UIM screens, and used for display only, without affecting eligibility, entitlement, or financial processing. Advises designing rules “backwards” from how case workers will navigate and view results, defining display categories, tab order, and a first summary category. Notes that display pages may include non-eligibility data such as sanction recommendations. Includes an example of configured categories for Incapacity Benefit.

Describes how to define display-rule requirements for product delivery cases, alongside eligibility and financial rule setup. The page explains when an Evidence Management wizard is suitable for change capture: multiple evidence types, sequenced updates, or conditional evidence based on captured information. It also outlines that product delivery cases hold the information and functions needed to determine benefit eligibility and deliver benefits, and that application rules/eligibility configurations must be defined and assigned to products, using Incapacity Benefit as the example.

children.title: (deprecated) Defining key decision factor rule requirements
children.summary: This deprecated section explains how to design decision display rules and optionally key decision factor rules in Cúram Express Rules. It stresses defining decision categories, display order, and product-specific UIM screens for showing calculation details, with the first category used by default. It recommends designing display rules “backwards” from the user experience and keeping them separate from eligibility/entitlement logic. It also introduces key decision factors as optional data points that help explain eligibility/entitlement results, such as income or medical evidence.

Explains how to assign Cúram Express Rules to products for eligibility, entitlement, and display behavior. It covers when an Evidence Management wizard is appropriate for change capture, how product delivery cases support benefit delivery, and how application rules must be defined and assigned to products. For Incapacity Benefit, the rule set includes non-financial, contribution, medical certificate, and assistance unit rules, with Sickness Benefit typically paid for six months before Invalidity Benefit. Also outlines display rule categories/UIM screens and notes key decision factors as deprecated/optional.

children.title: Defining eligibility determination requirements
Adult Social Care eligibility determination configurations
children.summary: This section explains eligibility determination configuration settings for Adult Social Care. It highlights default product-level settings: open-ended cases are allowed, all cases are automatically reassessed, decision summaries use “Total weekly monetary entitlement,” and determination comparison uses “Compare all user-facing data.” It also frames these settings as part of defining eligibility determination requirements and notes the role of key decision factors in displaying the data used in eligibility and entitlement results.

This section defines product-level eligibility determination settings: decision summary display strategy, determination comparison strategy, open-ended cases, and reassessment strategy. It then gives Adult Social Care defaults: open-ended cases allowed, all cases automatically reassessed, decision summaries shown as “Total weekly monetary entitlement,” and determinations compared using all user-facing data. It also introduces key decision factors as display-only data used to help case workers understand eligibility and entitlement results.

Defines product-level eligibility determination processing for Cúram Express Rules. Covers assigning rules to products for eligibility/entitlement, Adult Social Care application rules for Incapacity Benefit (non-financial, contribution, medical certificate, assistance unit), and default Sickness Benefit then Invalidity Benefit after six months. Also explains display rules for decision details, using product-specific UIM screens and tab categories, with display data not affecting financial processing. Key decision factors are described as deprecated/optional display-only data. Finally, it lists eligibility determination settings: open-ended cases allowed, compare all user-facing data, automatically reassess all cases, and summary display as total weekly monetary entitlement.

children.title: Defining the financial processes
Adult Social Care financial processing
children.summary: Explains Adult Social Care financial processing and how benefit payments are generated from eligibility-based financial schedules using Cúram Financials. Covers core financial objects (components, instruction line items, instructions, instruments) and their role in payments, liabilities, receipts, and cancellations. Also defines payment generation requirements: delivery patterns (frequency, cover pattern, method, offsets), and financial calendars for payment exclusion dates by method (Check, EFT, Cash, Voucher). Includes example default delivery pattern for Incapacity Benefit and timing considerations for nominees.

children.title: Defining payment generation requirements
Defining delivery pattern requirements
children.summary: Explains how to define payment delivery pattern requirements for Adult Social Care financial processing. A delivery pattern specifies payment/bill frequency, cover pattern (advance, arrears, once-off), and method, with offsets for processing lead time. Example: weekly check payments in advance on Monday; the Incapacity Benefit default is weekly cash in advance on Monday. Also introduces financial calendars as exclusion-date schedules for payment methods (Check, EFT, Cash, Voucher) to account for non-payment days such as holidays.

children.title: Defining financial calendar requirements
children.summary: This section explains how to define financial calendar requirements for payment processing. It says financial calendars store payment exclusion dates, which are days when payments cannot be made using a specific delivery method. Example: if a Monday cash payment falls on a public holiday, that day is excluded. Separate calendars are maintained for Check, EFT, Cash, and Voucher. This section is useful if you need to configure or understand how delivery-method-specific holidays affect payment generation dates.

This section explains how to define payment generation requirements in Adult Social Care financial processing. It says payment dates are affected by delivery patterns, offsets, and financial calendars. Delivery patterns specify frequency, cover pattern (advance, arrears, once-off), and method, with examples such as weekly check payments in advance on Monday and the default Incapacity Benefit pattern of weekly cash in advance on Monday. Financial calendars store exclusion dates for payment methods (Check, EFT, Cash, Voucher) to handle non-payment days such as holidays.

Explains how Adult Social Care defines financial processing for benefit payments. It covers Cúram Financials building blocks (financial components, instruction line items, instructions, instruments) used for payments, liabilities, receipts, and cancellations. It also describes how to set payment generation requirements using delivery patterns, offsets, and financial calendars. Delivery patterns define frequency, cover pattern, and method; examples include weekly check payments in advance and the default Incapacity Benefit pattern of weekly cash in advance on Monday. Financial calendars store exclusion dates for Check, EFT, Cash, and Voucher.

Covers how to analyze product delivery case requirements for benefit eligibility and payment processing. Defines a product delivery case as the container for eligibility and benefit delivery functions. Explains assigning Cúram Express Rules to products, documenting Adult Social Care Incapacity Benefit rules (non-financial, contribution, medical certificate, assistance unit), and the default Sickness Benefit for six months before Invalidity Benefit. Also describes display rules and optional key decision factors as display-only, eligibility settings, and financial processing basics: Cúram Financials components, delivery patterns, offsets, and financial calendars.

Covers case management analysis for integrated and product delivery cases. For integrated cases, define associated evidence types, correction vs succession updates via Effective Date of Change, verification requirements, brokering/sharing, and ownership strategy; Adult Social Care examples include Practitioner Certificate, ASC Diagnosis, Incapacity Benefit Product Type, and household members. For product delivery cases, assign Cúram Express Rules, document Incapacity Benefit eligibility rules, display rules, eligibility determination settings, and financial processing basics such as delivery patterns, offsets, and financial calendars.

This section guides analysis of the Adult Social Care application against customer requirements. It covers fit-gap analysis using Fiona’s practitioner certificate and phone-number examples, including gaps in evidence fields, labels, and code tables, and introduces CADT for generating application-specific analysis docs. It also outlines analyzing organization/location structure, user/security and UI requirements, intake and case-management requirements, and product rules, eligibility/determination, display rules, and financial processing settings for Incapacity Benefit, including delivery patterns and financial calendars.
